name: Automate a multi-step process with HTTP / REST API and MySQL in real time

parameters.content:
### Hourly Schedule

Starts the workflow on a timer — every 6 h. No input data; downstream nodes fetch what they need.

Next: Route by Work Type

### Route by Work Type

Routes each item to one of 3 lanes: sync, async + fallback.

Ports: ①→ Process 'sync' → MySQL · ②→ Fast-Track 'async' + Alert · ③→ Hold Unknown Types (DLQ)

### Process 'sync' → MySQL

Inserts a row into the MySQL table using fields auto-mapped from the incoming item.

Next: Merge Routed Work

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 MySQL connection

### Fast-Track 'async' + Alert

Sends a Telegram message to the configured chat — the same bot can also receive replies, so this doubles as a two-way ops channel.

Next: Merge Routed Work

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 Telegram bot token

### Hold Unknown Types (DLQ)

Inserts a row into the MySQL table using fields auto-mapped from the incoming item.

Next: Merge Routed Work

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 MySQL connection

### Merge Routed Work

Rejoins 3 branches into one stream (mode: append) so downstream logic runs once.

Next: Align Field Names

### Align Field Names

Normalizes field names so every source feeds the same downstream schema.

# Automate a multi-step process with HTTP / REST API and MySQL in real time

**Pattern:** Workflow Automation · **Level:** Foundation · **Trigger:** Scheduled

## What it does
Automate a multi-step process with HTTP / REST API and MySQL in real time. It runs automatically on a schedule.

## Why it exists
Manual summarization is slow and error-prone; this workflow makes the process repeatable, monitored and hands-off, with failures surfaced instead of silently lost.

## Main flow
Hourly Schedule → Route by Work Type → Process 'sync' → MySQL → Merge Routed Work → Align Field Names

## Use it for
- Teams that want to workflow automation without manual effort
- Anyone automating summarization in ai
- Replacing a fragile manual process with a reliable workflow

## You get
- Standardizes the process every time
- Has retries and error handling built in
- Scales without extra headcount

## Before first run
Connect credentials: MySQL connection; Telegram bot token.
Follow the color key: 🟦 entry · 🟪 AI · 🟫 actions · ⬜ utilities · 🟩 outputs · 🟥 error rail.
Each node has its own doc card underneath — read those before editing.

## Try it now
Sample data is pinned on the trigger — hit **Execute Workflow** to watch it flow through every node before connecting any real credentials. Unpin the trigger (right-click → Unpin) when you're ready to go live.

## Design decisions

Unrecognized work types are parked in a dead-letter lane instead of erroring the whole run or silently dropping the item — new event kinds will show up eventually, and this is where you'd notice one without losing it. One kind is deliberately routed through a sub-workflow to show how heavier per-type logic can be extracted without touching this router at all.

## Practice exercises

New to this pattern? Try these in order:

1. Send a work-type value that matches no switch rule and confirm it lands in the dead-letter branch.
2. Add a new work type and its own handling branch.
3. Replace the direct-write branch with a sub-workflow call, matching the pattern already used for the first kind.

## 🛟 When this fails

This starter workflow keeps things simple: no automatic retry or error branch yet. If a node fails, the execution just stops — open n8n's **Executions** tab to see which node and why. Ready to harden it? Turn on **Retry On Fail** (3 tries, 2s backoff) on the Settings tab of each external node, and add an error-output branch to a Slack/email alert — every Production-tier template in this library shows that exact pattern.

## 🛠 Customize me

Quick wins: point the data nodes at your own accounts (see 🔑 lines on each card), adjust the trigger cadence, and tune thresholds/labels to your summarization process. Every node card explains its role — safe to extend at any marked branch.
